truth.papers.A7.title: Factors influencing austenite stability during Inter-critical annealing and effect on mechanical properties of low-density medium Mn steels
truth.papers.A7.paper_scope: Investigates addition of 3% Al with and without 1% Si to Fe-8Mn-0.2C medium-Mn steel to improve strength-ductility combination and understand austenite stability during intercritical annealing.

prediction.papers.A7.title: Factors influencing austenite stability during Inter-critical annealing and effect on mechanical properties of low-density medium Mn steels
prediction.papers.A7.paper_scope: Investigate the effect of adding 3% Al with and without 1% Si to Fe-8Mn-0.2C medium-Mn steel on austenite stability during intercritical annealing and the resulting mechanical properties, microstructural evolution, and transformation kinetics.

truth.alloys.alloy_fe8mn3al02c.alloys_notes: Referred to as M1; composition Fe–8Mn–3Al-0.2C (all in wt.%).
truth.alloys.alloy_fe8mn3al1si02c.alloys_notes: Referred to as M2; composition Fe–8Mn–3Al–1Si-0.2C (all in wt.%).

prediction.alloys.alloy_fe8mn3al02c.alloys_notes: Maximum Al addition without equilibrium δ-ferrite formation in the Fe-8Mn-0.2C system.
prediction.alloys.alloy_fe8mn3al1si02c.alloys_notes: Addition of 1% Si leads to equilibrium δ-ferrite presence in the intercritical temperature range.

truth.processes.proc_M1_HR.description: Homogenization at 1100°C for 2 h followed by water quenching; hot rolling at 1100°C with ~50% thickness reduction, followed by air cooling.
truth.processes.proc_M2_HR.description: Homogenization at 1100°C for 2 h followed by water quenching; hot rolling at 1100°C with ~50% thickness reduction, followed by air cooling.
truth.processes.proc_M1_IA.description: Homogenization at 1100°C for 2 h, water quenching; hot rolling at 1100°C with ~50% reduction, air cooling; intercritical annealing at 700°C for 1 h, water quenching.
truth.processes.proc_M2_IA.description: Homogenization at 1100°C for 2 h, water quenching; hot rolling at 1100°C with ~50% reduction, air cooling; intercritical annealing at 700°C for 1 h, water quenching.

prediction.processes.proc_M1_IA.description: Vacuum arc melting with four remelts, homogenization at 1100°C for 2 h followed by water quenching, hot rolling at 1100°C with ~50% thickness reduction and finish rolling around 900°C followed by air cooling, and intercritical annealing at 700°C for 1 h followed by water quenching.
prediction.processes.proc_M1_IA.processes_notes: Ingot flipped before each remelt to ensure uniformity. Intercritical annealing temperature selected based on thermodynamic predictions to maximize austenite fraction while maintaining Mn concentration ~11% in austenite.
prediction.processes.proc_M2_IA.description: Vacuum arc melting with four remelts, homogenization at 1100°C for 2 h followed by water quenching, hot rolling at 1100°C with ~50% thickness reduction and finish rolling around 900°C followed by air cooling, and intercritical annealing at 700°C for 1 h followed by water quenching.
prediction.processes.proc_M2_IA.processes_notes: Ingot flipped before each remelt to ensure uniformity. Intercritical annealing temperature selected based on thermodynamic predictions to maximize austenite fraction while maintaining Mn concentration ~11% in austenite.

prediction.processing_steps.sample_8mn3al02c_HQ.pro: Applied to as-cast ingot followed by water quenching.
prediction.processing_steps.sample_M1_IA.processing: Selected based on equilibrium austenite fraction and critical Mn concentration in austenite.
prediction.processing_steps.sample_M2_IA.processing: Selected based on equilibrium austenite fraction and critical Mn concentration in austenite.

truth.structures.sample_M1_HR.overall_structur: Multiple phases: polygonal α-ferrite and lath-type martensitic-austenitic (α'+γ) phase; retained austenite fraction ~5.1%.
truth.structures.sample_M2_HR.overall_structur: Consists of δ-ferrite, α-ferrite, and very fine martensitic-austenitic (α'+γ) phase; retained austenite fraction ~5.7%.
truth.structures.sample_M1_IA.overall_structur: Mainly α-ferrite and austenite with negligible amount of martensite; retained austenite fraction ~45%.
truth.structures.sample_M1_IA.microstructure_l: Contains austenite grains both smaller and larger than 0.5 μm; a high fraction of grains < 0.5 μm contributes to higher austenite stability.
truth.structures.sample_M2_IA.overall_structur: α-ferrite, austenite, and coarse band-type δ-ferrite; retained austenite fraction ~19%.
truth.structures.sample_M2_IA.microstructure_l: Contains some austenite grains < 0.5 μm, but fraction of fine grains lower than in M1-IA, leading to lower overall austenite stability.

prediction.structures.sample_M1_HR.overall_structur: Multiple phases including polygonal α-ferrite and lath-type martensitic-austenitic (α'+γ) phase.
prediction.structures.sample_M2_HR.overall_structur: Multiple phases including δ-ferrite, α-ferrite, and very fine martensitic-austenitic (α'+γ) phase.
prediction.structures.sample_M1_IA.overall_structur: Dual phase microstructure composed of α-ferrite and austenite with negligible martensite.
prediction.structures.sample_M2_IA.overall_structur: Microstructure comprising α-ferrite, austenite, and coarse band-type δ-ferrite with negligible martensite.

truth.interfaces.sample_M1_IA.phase_evolution: During intercritical annealing, Mn and C partition from ferrite to austenite across the α/γ interface, leading to local enrichment of Mn in austenite near the interface. This partitioning, along with differences in grain size, contributes to higher-than-expected austenite stability.
truth.interfaces.sample_M2_IA.phase_evolution: Mn and C partition from both α-ferrite and δ-ferrite to austenite; Si partitions from austenite to δ-ferrite and α-ferrite. These partitioning effects influence austenite stability and explain the discrepancy between observed and equilibrium-predicted austenite fractions.

prediction.interfaces.sample_M1_IA.phase_evolution: Mn partitions from α-ferrite to austenite during intercritical annealing; local Mn enrichment occurs due to finite annealing time and insufficient diffusion, leading to concentration gradients across the moving interface.
prediction.interfaces.sample_M2_IA.phase_evolution: Mn and C partition from ferrite into austenite; Si additionally partitions from austenite into both δ-ferrite and α-ferrite during intercritical annealing.

prediction.performance.sample_M1_IA.comparative_per: PSE 61 GPa% vs 41 GPa% for M2-IA
prediction.performance.sample_M2_IA.comparative_per: Lower PSE 41 GPa% vs 61 GPa% for M1-IA

truth.characterization_methods.sample_M1_HR.pu: Phase identification and volume fraction measurement
truth.characterization_methods.sample_M1_HR.ke: Confirmed ferrite/martensite and austenite phases; austenite volume fraction = 5.1%.
truth.characterization_methods.sample_M2_HR.pu: Phase identification and volume fraction measurement
truth.characterization_methods.sample_M2_HR.ke: Confirmed ferrite/martensite and austenite phases; austenite volume fraction = 5.7%.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_10.purpose: Measure tensile properties at room temperature
truth.characterization_methods.idx_10.key_find: Engineering stress-strain curves obtained; UTS, elongation, and PSE reported for M1-HR, M2-HR, M1-IA, M2-IA.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_11.purpose: Evaluate retained austenite fraction and strain distribution after deformation
truth.characterization_methods.idx_11.key_find: Post-tensile austenite fractions: M1-IA 6.5%, M2-IA 3.2%; KAM maps show increased misorientation and strain partitioning in M2.

prediction.characterization_methods.sample_M1_HR.pu: Evaluate mechanical properties at room temperature
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_M1_HR.ke: UTS 1712±10 MPa, total elongation 14.8±0.5%, strain rate 10^-3 s^-1.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_M2_HR.pu: Evaluate mechanical properties at room temperature
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_M2_HR.ke: UTS 1055±15 MPa, total elongation 15.4±0.8%, strain rate 10^-3 s^-1.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_M1_IA.pu: Evaluate local deformation and phase transformation extent via kernel average misorientation
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_M1_IA.ke: Significant increase in KAM values after tensile testing, indicating extensive austenite to martensite transformation.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_M2_IA.pu: Evaluate local deformation and phase transformation extent via kernel average misorientation
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_M2_IA.ke: Significant increase in KAM after tensile testing with strain partitioning into low KAM (δ-ferrite) and high KAM (transformed martensite) regions.

truth.computational_details.idx_0.validation_a: Predicted austenite fractions at IA temperature compared with measured retained austenite; discrepancies attributed to martensite formation during cooling and local microstructural heterogeneity.
truth.computational_details.idx_1.simulation_m: Martensite start temperature (Ms) calculation and Koistinen-Marburger model
truth.computational_details.idx_1.validation_a: Calculated retained austenite fractions at RT using Ms and K-M model underestimated observed fractions, indicating additional stabilizing factors.
truth.computational_details.idx_1.analytical_m: Compositions in wt.% of austenite at respective temperatures
Used to compute Ms for hot-rolled and IA conditions.
Ms from above equation; T = room temperature (taken as ~25°C)
Used to estimate fraction of austenite transformed to martensite upon cooling; critical IAT above which retained austenite is lower than equilibrium predicted.
truth.computational_details.idx_2.analytical_m: Vγ = (1/A Σ Iγj/Rγj) / [1/B Σ Iαj/Rαj + 1/A Σ Iγj/Rγj]
Integrated intensities of γ and α peaks, theoretical intensities
Used to calculate austenite volume fraction from XRD data.
truth.computational_details.idx_3.analytical_m: Exponential decay model for austenite fraction during strain
A = initial austenite fraction, B fitted as 3.6 (M1), 3.8 (M2)
Lower B indicates higher mechanical stability and slower TRIP kinetics.

prediction.computational_details.idx_0.validation_a: Predicted intercritical temperature window broadened with Al and Si additions. Predicted equilibrium austenite fractions at 700°C were approximately 48-50% for M1 and 30-32% for M2. Observed retained austenite fractions after IA were 45% and 19% respectively, indicating partial martensitic transformation during cooling not captured by equilibrium predictions alone.
prediction.computational_details.idx_1.simulation_m: Empirical martensite-start temperature calculation and Koistinen-Marburger model
prediction.computational_details.idx_1.validation_a: Calculated Ms temperatures for hot-rolled samples were 319°C (M1) and 296°C (M2). For IA conditions, the K-M model predicted retained austenite fractions of approximately 16% for M1 and 11% for M2 at room temperature, which are significantly lower than observed values of 45% and 19%, suggesting additional local microstructural heterogeneity factors enhance austenite stability.
prediction.computational_details.idx_1.analytical_m: Calculate martensite start temperature from austenite composition
Ms(°C) = 539 - 423C - 30.4Mn - 7.5Si + 30Al, with elemental concentrations in wt.%
Austenite composition at the relevant processing temperature used as input.
Predict retained austenite fraction as a function of undercooling below Ms
Applied to calculate retained austenite at room temperature using calculated Ms.
prediction.computational_details.idx_2.simulation_m: Mechanical stability fitting of retained austenite transformation kinetics during tensile deformation
prediction.computational_details.idx_2.validation_a: Fitting parameter B obtained as 3.6 for M1 and 3.8 for M2. Lower B for M1 indicates higher mechanical stability and slower TRIP kinetics, consistent with higher retained austenite fraction and finer grains.
prediction.computational_details.idx_2.analytical_m: Exponential decay model for austenite transformation
Model austenite volume fraction as a function of true strain during tensile testing
f_γ = A × e^{-Bε}; B = 3.6 for M1, B = 3.8 for M2
A represents initial austenite fraction before tensile test.

truth.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Si-free steel (M1-IA) achieved PSE of 61 GPa% vs. 41 GPa% for Si-containing steel (M2-IA).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Stacking fault energy (SFE) of both steels is below 18 mJ/m², favoring TRIP effect during deformation.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Austenite stability is influenced by local Mn enrichment due to insufficient diffusion during 1 h IA, leading to higher-than-expected Mn in some grains (>12%).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Grain size heterogeneity: austenite grains < 0.5 µm exhibit higher stability due to finer size and higher carbon concentration.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: In M2, Si partitions from austenite to δ-ferrite and α-ferrite during IA, affecting austenite composition and stability.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Critical IAT exists above which full retention of equilibrium austenite is not expected; for M1 at 700°C, calculated retained austenite was only ~16%, but observed was 45%, explained by additional stabilizing factors.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: For M2-IA, calculated retained austenite ~11% vs. observed ~19%, also explained by local heterogeneity but to a lesser extent due to lower fraction of fine grains.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Fractography: HR samples show cleavage fracture (brittle), IA samples show dimple rupture (ductile).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: Strain hardening rate curves show three stages; stage II gradual decline corresponds to TRIP effect, with end values 1338 MPa (M1-IA) and 989 MPa (M2-IA).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_9.value: Post-tensile EBSD phase maps show austenite reduction from 45% to 6.5% (M1) and from 19% to 3.2% (M2), confirming TRIP.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_10.value: δ-ferrite in M2 introduces additional partitioning of Mn and C into austenite, contributing to stability but also leads to strain partitioning observed in KAM maps.

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Product of strength and elongation (PSE) values: 61 GPa% for M1-IA and 41 GPa% for M2-IA.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Retained austenite fractions after tensile fracture determined by EBSD: 6.5% for M1-IA and 3.2% for M2-IA, confirming deformation-induced martensitic transformation (TRIP effect).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Stacking fault energy (SFE) values of both steels are below 18 mJ/m², which is the critical SFE for TRIP effect occurrence.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Calculated Ms temperatures for hot-rolled austenite compositions: 319°C for M1 and 296°C for M2, supporting martensite formation during air cooling after hot rolling.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Grain size distribution analysis (Fig. 8b) indicates M1-IA contains a higher fraction of austenite grains smaller than 0.5 μm compared to M2-IA, contributing to higher local austenite stability.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Fig. 7 XRD data table indicates 45.6% austenite for M1-IA, whereas abstract and main text consistently report 45%; the main text value is used as the authoritative figure.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Dog-bone-shaped tensile specimens had a gauge length of 2 mm, thickness of 1 mm, and width of 1 mm, machined by wire-EDM.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Tensile testing was performed on a Tinus-Olsen ST25 universal testing machine equipped with a 25 kN load cell.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: Fig. 11 contains true stress, strain hardening rate, and retained austenite fraction versus true strain plots; precise numerical coordinates beyond point A (1338 MPa) and point B (989 MPa) are not provided in the text.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_9.value: Engineering stress-strain curves and fractography images are provided in Fig. 10; HR samples exhibit brittle cleavage with river markings, while IA samples exhibit ductile dimple rupture.
